title: Arrays
description: Storing many values under one name: declaring an array, zero-based indexing, looping through it, and the out-of-bounds trap.
objectives: Loop through an array with a for loop
Understand why C++ doesn't check array bounds for you

sections.text: 1. The Problem: Many Related Variables

Five separate variables for five scores is manageable. A hundred scores this way would be unmanageable — and there'd be no way to loop through them, since a for loop can't iterate over separately named variables.
2. What is an Array?
An array is a fixed-size collection of values of the same type, stored together and accessed using a single name and a position number called an index.
One name, scores, holding all five values. [5] tells the compiler how many elements to reserve room for.
3. Zero-Based Indexing
C++ arrays are zero-indexed: the first element is at index 0, not 1. An array of 5 elements has valid indices 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — there is no index 5. This trips up nearly every beginner at least once.
4. Full Example
5. Looping Through an Array
This is exactly why zero-based indexing pairs naturally with for loops: i starts at 0 and runs while i < 5, matching the array's valid indices precisely.
6. Modifying an Element

sections.code: scores[2] = 100; // overwrite the element at index 2 (was 78)

sections.text: Each element behaves like an individual variable — it can be read or reassigned using its index, exactly like scores[2] = 100; here.
7. Declaring Without Initializing

sections.code: int numbers[5]; // size given, but no values yet — contents are garbage, like an uninitialized variable

sections.text: Just like an uninitialized variable from Variables, an array declared without values contains garbage — unpredictable leftover data. Always initialize before reading.
8. Out-of-Bounds Access — No Safety Net

C++ does not check whether an index is actually valid. scores[5] doesn't throw a clean error — it reads whatever memory happens to sit right after the array, which is undefined behavior: it might print garbage, might crash, or might even appear to "work" by accident. This is one of the most notorious sources of bugs in C++, and there is no automatic protection against it.
9. Array Size Is Fixed

Once declared with int scores[5];, that array can never hold more or fewer than 5 elements — its size is fixed for its entire lifetime. Growable, resizable collections are covered later, once we reach vector in the STL.
10. Real-Life Example

sections.title: A row of numbered lockers
sections.text: Think of an array as a row of lockers, numbered starting from 0, all reserved together as one block. scores[2] means "open locker number 2" — a fixed, direct way to reach exactly one item without touching the others. Try to open locker number 5 in a row of 5 lockers (0 through 4), and there's nothing there — but nothing stops you from reaching for it anyway.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting arrays are zero-indexed — the last valid index is size - 1, not size.
Accessing an out-of-bounds index — C++ does not check this for you, and the result is undefined behavior, not a clean error.
Looping with i <= 5 instead of i < 5 for a 5-element array — an off-by-one that reads one element past the end.
Assuming an array can grow — its size is fixed once declared; a different tool (vector, covered later) is needed for resizable storage.
Reading an array element before initializing it — like any uninitialized variable, it holds garbage.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is an array?
questions.answer: A fixed-size collection of values of the same type, stored together and accessed by a single name with an index.

questions.question: What is the index of the first element of an array?
What is the last valid index of an array with 10 elements?
What happens if you access an array index that's out of bounds?
questions.answer: C++ does not check bounds automatically; accessing an invalid index is undefined behavior, which can silently read garbage, crash, or appear to work unpredictably.

questions.question: Can an array be resized after it's declared?
questions.answer: No — a C++ array's size is fixed at declaration; a resizable alternative like vector is needed for growable storage.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: An array is a fixed-size collection of values of the same type, stored together under one name and accessed by an index. C++ arrays are zero-indexed, so an array of size n has valid indices 0 through n-1, which pairs naturally with a for loop like for (int i = 0; i < n; i++). Critically, C++ does not check array bounds — accessing an out-of-range index is undefined behavior rather than a clean error, making off-by-one mistakes a classic and dangerous bug. An array's size is also fixed once declared; resizable collections come later with vector.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Arrays
sections.text: Array = a row of lockers starting at 0. Last valid locker = size - 1. Nothing stops you from reaching for locker 'size' — but there's nothing there.
